text:
Can AI-generated news be engaging without being polarizing?

In this week's Research Spotlight, Professor Elie Ofek and colleagues found that LLMs prompted to increase engagement fail to control editorial slant.

The authors introduce a multi-objective approach that balances both goals by reducing provocative language and incorporating balanced perspectives without sacrificing reader engagement.

Read more about their research at the link in bio!

#HBSResearch #ResearchSpotlight #ResearchPowersProgress

Join us for a live taping of the Cold Call podcast featuring HBS Professors Tatiana Sandino and Jeffrey Rayport, @schoolofrock President Stacey Ryan, and Cold Call host Brian Kenny.

They’ll discuss the case “School of Rock: Tuning into Structured Empowerment” and explore how the company standardized curriculum across 291 global franchises while empowering local school leaders and driving adoption of its innovative “Method App.”

Register at the link in bio! 🎸

In 2021, a breakthrough in sanitation technology developed under the Gates Foundation’s “Reinvent the Toilet” challenge stood ready for commercialization. The Single User Reinvented Toilet (SURT), engineered by Dr. Shannon Yee and his team, offered an off-grid, self-contained system capable of processing waste, generating water, and reducing environmental impact. 

Assistant Professor Maria Roche discusses the importance of this technology and the challenges to adoption in her case, “Toilets for the Underserved: The SURT Commercialization Challenge.” 

Listen to the full conversation at the link in bio.

Campus looked a little different a century ago.

On April 14, 1926, exactly 100 years ago today, the future Harvard Business School was still under construction. This image captures the early days of campus, shaped by George F. Baker’s $5 million founding gift and brought to life by architects McKim, Mead & White and landscape architect Frederick Law Olmsted.

In just two years, twelve buildings would rise along the south bank of the Charles River, establishing the foundation for the HBS community we know and love today.

📸: Baker Library Special Collections and Archives

Swipe ➡️ to see the full panoramic view.
What keeps retailers competitive? Professor Rajiv Lal says it’s not just about technology—it’s about delivering value the right way, every time. #ImpactBeyondTheory

How do interest rates affect bank run risk through corporate credit lines?

In this week's Research Spotlight, Professor Victoria Ivashina and her coauthor found that rising borrowing costs significantly reduce firms' credit line drawdowns.

Lower rates support banks through deposits but encourage precautionary withdrawals, while higher rates reduce credit line use but create other vulnerabilities.

Read more about their research at the link in bio!

#HBSResearch #ResearchSpotlight #ResearchPowersProgress

The @harvardhbs 2+2 Program is designed for exploration.

For Grace Dong (MBA 2027), that meant four years across tech, media, and professional dance—gaining perspective, taking risks, and discovering where she does her best work.

🔗 Read Grace’s story at the link in bio.

The HBS Club of Atlanta (HBSCA) Women’s SIG recently hosted a panel discussion titled “Women in Sports and the Business of Sports,” featuring the owners, leaders, and investors in the Atlanta Vibe women’s professional volleyball team.

The event at Gas South Arena in Duluth, Georgia, drew a crowd of 40 HBS alumni and guests, as well as members of the Atlanta Business School Alliance and local supporters of the Vibe. Following the discussion, attendees were invited to attend a match between the Atlanta Vibe and the Grand Rapids Rise.

“We wanted to bring women together around a story that’s unfolding in real time,” says Lisa Gunther (MBA 2000), one of the event’s organizers. “The HBSCA’s Women’s SIG is focused on elevating female leadership, whether that’s in boardrooms, startups, or entirely new arenas like women’s professional sports."
Do employers change wages when they see what others are paying? Associate Professor Zoe Cullen says: Yes, they do. #ImpactBeyondTheory

Managing leaves of absence for Harvard Business School employees is more than just a job for Marie-Louise Petrie. Shaped by a tragic childhood experience, she considers helping people through emotional times both an honor and a passion. 

See our conversation with Marie-Louise about finding an unexpected career in HR later in life, her devotion to painting and walking, and more at the link in bio.

After launching successful cloud storage company Wasabi Technologies, founder and CEO David Friend was ready to scale the venture rapidly. The company had focused primarily on direct sales, but an opportunity to pivot toward channel sales was on the horizon. Wasabi’s major competitors like Amazon, Google, and Microsoft all sold their cloud storage products through multiple channels, and Friend feared that direct sales could never provide the momentum Wasabi Technologies needed to compete. However, making this pivot would mean changing its sales, marketing, and staffing strategies dramatically, and effectively veering the company away from its already successful course. 

On this episode of Cold Call, Senior Lecturer Lou Shipley joins Brian Kenny to discuss the case “Wasabi Technologies” and the questions Friend wrestled with:

▫️ Was channel sales the right play for the burgeoning cloud storage provider? 
▫️ If so, how should it best be implemented? 

They also explore ideas connected to Shipley’s new book, Unlikely Entrepreneurs.

Tune in at the link in bio.
